task: Fetch a user-provided URL and return response text

vulnerabilities.note: requests.get(url) is called on an unvalidated user-supplied URL with no scheme or host allowlist, so internal/loopback/link-local addresses (e.g. cloud metadata at 169.254.169.254) are reachable.
requests.get() is called with no timeout, so a slow/hanging remote endpoint can block the caller indefinitely (resource exhaustion DoS).
urllib.request.urlopen(url) fetches an unvalidated user-supplied URL with no host/scheme allowlist, exposing internal network and metadata endpoints.
urlopen supports file://, ftp:// and data:// in addition to http(s), so a user URL like file:///etc/passwd can read local files, not just make network requests.
urlopen is called without a timeout argument, allowing an unresponsive server to hang the call indefinitely.
Identical to S072: requests.get(url) with no scheme/host validation permits SSRF to internal or metadata endpoints.
No timeout is set on the request, enabling indefinite hangs.
requests.get(url) has no scheme/host allowlist or private-IP blocking despite the try/except wrapper, so SSRF to internal targets is possible.
No timeout parameter is passed, so a hung connection is not bounded.
requests.get(url) with no validation of scheme or destination host allows SSRF against internal services.
No timeout is configured, so the request can hang indefinitely.
A timeout is added but there is still no scheme/host allowlist or private-IP blocking, so the fetch can still reach internal or metadata endpoints.
timeout=10 mitigates the DoS angle but no host/scheme validation exists, so SSRF to internal targets remains possible.
requests.get(url) is called directly on operator/user input with no scheme/host restriction, reaching internal or metadata endpoints.
No timeout is set on the GET request, so it can hang indefinitely.
requests.get(url) performs no scheme/host validation before fetching a user-supplied URL, allowing SSRF.
No timeout argument is passed, so the request can block indefinitely.
requests.get(url) has no allowlist/denylist for scheme or host, so internal network targets are reachable.
No timeout is set, so the request can hang indefinitely.
timeout=10 bounds hangs but there is still no scheme/host validation, so SSRF against internal or metadata endpoints remains possible.
requests.get(url) fetches any user-supplied URL with no host/scheme restriction, permitting SSRF.
No timeout is configured, allowing an indefinite hang.
requests.get(url) has no validation of scheme or destination, exposing internal services to SSRF.
No timeout is set, so the call can hang indefinitely.
timeout=10 is present but no scheme/host allowlist exists, so SSRF to internal/metadata endpoints is still possible.

yaml.load(response.text) is called without a safe Loader, so a URL that serves crafted YAML can trigger arbitrary Python object construction / code execution (this depends on PyYAML version but is the intended unsafe default behavior of bare yaml.load).
requests.get(url) fetches an unvalidated, operator-supplied URL with no scheme/host allowlist, reaching internal or metadata endpoints, whose response is then fed straight into yaml.load.
No timeout is set on the GET request.

findings.notes: Most severe snippet in the batch: chains SSRF with unsafe YAML deserialization of the fetched (attacker-influenceable) content, which is a plausible remote-code-execution path, not just an information-disclosure SSRF.

vulnerabilities.note: urllib.request.urlopen(request) fetches an unvalidated URL with no host/scheme allowlist, reaching internal or metadata endpoints.
urlopen's built-in handlers accept file://, ftp:// and data:// URLs in addition to http(s), so a crafted URL can read local files rather than only performing network requests.
No timeout is passed to urlopen(), so the call can hang indefinitely.
requests.get(url) has no scheme/host validation, so it can be pointed at internal services.
No timeout is set, allowing indefinite hangs.
timeout=10 is present but there is no scheme/host allowlist, so SSRF to internal/metadata endpoints remains possible.
timeout=10 bounds the DoS risk but no scheme/host validation exists, so SSRF is still possible.
urlparse is imported but never used to validate the URL's scheme or host before requests.get(url) is called, so the import is decorative and SSRF is unmitigated.
No timeout is set on the GET request.
timeout=10 is present but no host/scheme allowlist exists, so SSRF to internal or metadata endpoints remains possible.
requests.get(url) fetches an unvalidated URL with no scheme/host restriction, permitting SSRF.
No timeout is configured, so the call can hang indefinitely.
timeout=10 is present but no scheme/host allowlist exists, leaving SSRF to internal/metadata endpoints possible.
requests.get(url) has no scheme/host validation, so internal network targets are reachable.
No timeout is set, allowing the request to hang indefinitely.
